channel.title: Education on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar
channel.description: Recent content in Education on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar

item.title: Students Flash Red Cards at Adam Adli Over Broken UUCA Promise — PH's Own Poster Boy Turns Reneger
item.description:
Said by: Adam Adli Abdul Halim, Deputy Higher Education Minister

Category: Education

Date: 2026-02-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 February 2026, university students marched to Parliament demanding the abolition of the Universities and University Colleges Act 1971 (UUCA). Their target? Deputy Higher Education Minister Adam Adli Abdul Halim — once Malaysia’s most famous student activist, now the living embodiment of PH’s broken promise. Abolishing UUCA was a key manifesto promise by Pakatan Harapan in both GE14 (2018) and GE15 (2022). In late January 2026, Higher Education Minister Zambry Abd Kadir told Parliament the government had “no plans” to abolish UUCA — a policy reversal delivered without fanfare. Students flashed red cards at Adam Adli and called him a “reneger.” Zambry refused to receive their memorandum. Adam Adli, 33, was arrested in 2013 for lowering a flag at a student rally — an act that made him a symbol of the student movement. PH championed his case as proof that UUCA was unjust. Now he sits in the ministry that enforces it.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: PH promised to repeal UUCA in two consecutive elections. Four years into government, the Higher Education Minister says there are “no plans” to abolish it. This is not a delay — it is a lie. The promise was never intended to be kept; it was designed to harvest student votes. Adam Adli’s personal arc is the most devastating metaphor for the PH government: the student who was arrested for lowering a flag in protest now sits in the ministry that maintains the law that criminalises student protest. The students who voted for PH based on this promise are now protesting PH’s own deputy minister. Zambry’s refusal to receive the memorandum is a symbolic gut punch — when the government won’t even accept a piece of paper from student protesters, the message is clear: your vote was useful, your voice is not. UUCA determines whether 1.3 million Malaysian university students can participate in the political life of their country. Keeping it in place means PH believes students should vote for reform but not advocate for it.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Under UUCA, students can face suspension or expulsion for participating in political activities. The law was enacted in 1971 after student protests against rural poverty — it was designed from the start to suppress politically conscious youth. PH knew this. PH campaigned against it. PH’s own deputy minister was a victim of it. And now PH is keeping it. The Straits Times noted the protest “adds to Anwar’s pre-election reform challenges” — framing UUCA as part of a broader pattern of stalled promises eroding youth support. If you cannot keep promises to students who believed in you, the reform brand is dead.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: UUCA reform has been promised by every opposition coalition since the 2008 political tsunami. PH 1.0 (2018-2020) promised reform but collapsed before acting. PH 2.0 has now had over three years and explicitly reversed the promise. The pattern is identical to toll abolition, AG-PP separation, and the Sedition Act repeal: promise boldly in opposition, delay in government, quietly abandon. UUCA joins a growing list of manifesto pledges that were campaign decorations, not governance commitments.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your deputy minister was arrested under this law. He built his career fighting it. Now he defends the ministry that enforces it. If you wrote this as fiction, it would be criticised as too on-the-nose. Abolish UUCA or amend it to remove restrictions on student political activity — as you promised twice. The 1.3 million students in Malaysian universities are watching. And they vote.

Sources: Straits Times | Malaysiakini | FMT

item.title: Mandatory Malay and History Across ALL Education Systems — Critics Say It Stifles Diversity
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Education

Date: 2026-01-21

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In January 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced that Bahasa Melayu and History would be mandatory across all education systems in Malaysia — including vernacular schools (SJKC and SJKT), international schools, and private institutions. Anwar said the requirement aims to “bolster the status of Bahasa Melayu as the national language” and provide “a common understanding of national history.” Critics argued this undermines constitutional protections for vernacular education under Article 152(1)(a) and stifles the diversity that makes Malaysia’s education system unique. CNA’s in-depth analysis noted the “politics and difficulties” of Malaysian education reform, describing how “language and religion” remain the most combustible policy areas.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Most vernacular school students already learn Bahasa Melayu — it is a compulsory subject in all government-aided schools. The policy change is about expanding mandatory subjects into streams that have traditionally set their own academic priorities. Meanwhile, the actual education crisis goes completely unaddressed: PISA scores keep declining, tertiary enrollment is only 42.57% versus the global average of 51.98%, teachers report students are “ruder, more restless, less curious,” bullying cases surged 17%, and the education budget share has dropped from 6.8% to 4.3% of GDP. Adding mandatory subjects to already overcrowded curricula does not improve education quality — it increases workload without fixing pedagogy, infrastructure, or teacher training.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: National unity through compulsory curriculum is a BN-era idea wearing a PH-era suit. Mahathir pushed similar initiatives. So did Muhyiddin. The framing changes; the policy doesn’t. The political calculus is transparent: mandatory Malay and History plays well with the Malay-Muslim base ahead of elections. The educational merit is secondary to the political signalling. This is identity politics disguised as curriculum reform. The real issue isn’t whether students learn Malay — it’s whether the government is using “unity” rhetoric to homogenise education while ignoring the systemic failures that are actually destroying the system: 25% math failure rates, 10,160 SPM no-shows, and teachers overwhelmed by 30+ students per class with zero structural support.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian PM has used language and curriculum policy as a political tool. Mahathir’s Vision Schools initiative in the early 2000s attempted to co-locate vernacular and national schools. It was resisted and largely failed. The PPSMI policy (teaching math and science in English) was introduced, reversed, reintroduced as DLP, and remains a political football. The pattern is consistent: use education policy for political signalling, face backlash, retreat or water down, then try again under a different name. PH is simply the latest government to reach for the “national unity through mandatory curriculum” lever.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your education system is in freefall — declining PISA scores, surging bullying, overwhelmed teachers, students disengaged and scrolling under desks — and your response is to add more mandatory subjects? Fix the teaching first. Fund the schools properly. Address the 33% of students failing at least one SPM subject. Then talk about curriculum additions. Adding mandatory Malay and History to schools that already teach Malay while ignoring the collapse in teaching quality is not reform — it is deflection. Students don’t need more subjects. They need better education.

Sources: Vietnam News | CNA

item.title: Malaysia's Schooling System in 'Decline' — Students Ruder, Less Curious, Teachers Overwhelmed
item.description:
Said by: FMT Opinion / Teachers

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 18 October 2025, Free Malaysia Today published an opinion piece by frontline teachers describing a schooling system in visible decline. The accounts were blunt: students scrolling TikTok under desks during lessons, snapping back at teachers when corrected, refusing to cooperate with classroom activities, and showing little to no curiosity about what’s being taught.

Teachers described fighting a losing battle — not against students, but against the algorithm. TikTok’s dopamine loop has become the real classroom authority. And structurally, teachers have nothing to fight back with: no updated pedagogy, no device management policy, no mental health support, and no institutional acknowledgment that the problem even exists.

The piece painted a picture of a profession in quiet crisis — underpaid, under-respected, and now outgunned by a phone screen.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Malaysia’s education system was already in trouble before TikTok. PISA scores have been stagnant or declining for over a decade. The 2022 PISA results placed Malaysian students below the OECD average in reading, mathematics, and science — a pattern that has not meaningfully improved since Malaysia first participated.

What the FMT piece describes is not a new problem. It is the visible symptom of decades of systemic neglect:

  • Overcrowded classrooms with 35–40 students per teacher in many urban and semi-urban schools
  • Outdated curricula that prioritise rote memorisation over critical thinking
  • Teacher burnout driven by administrative overload, low pay, and zero autonomy
  • No digital literacy framework — students have devices but no structured guidance on using them productively
  • Parental abdication — the phone has become the babysitter, and schools are expected to fix what homes have abandoned The decline isn’t coming. It’s here. And no one in Putrajaya is treating it as a crisis.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek has spent her tenure announcing feel-good initiatives — coding programmes, STEM hubs, “joy of learning” campaigns — while the structural foundations of the system rot underneath.

On teacher welfare:

  • Teachers are buried in clerical work, data entry, and co-curricular admin that has nothing to do with teaching. The ministry’s response has been to promise “reducing teacher workload” — a promise made by every education minister since at least 2015. Nothing has changed. On classroom discipline:

  • There is no coherent national framework for managing device use in schools. Some schools ban phones outright; others ignore the issue entirely. The ministry has issued no binding directive, leaving every teacher to fend for themselves against a billion-dollar attention economy. On curriculum reform:

  • The national curriculum (KSSM) was last updated in 2017. It still emphasises exam performance over genuine learning. The system produces students who can pass SPM but cannot write a coherent paragraph, evaluate a source, or hold a sustained conversation about anything that isn’t on a screen. On funding:

  • Malaysia spends roughly 3.2% of GDP on education — below the global average of 4.3% and well below regional peers like Vietnam (4.1%) and Thailand (3.8%). PH promised to prioritise education spending. The 2025 and 2026 budgets tell a different story. The ministry’s approach is cosmetic. Announce a pilot programme. Take photos. Move on. Meanwhile, a generation of students is being failed in real time.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • 2022 PISA Results: Malaysia scored 398 in mathematics, 388 in reading, and 398 in science — all below the OECD average. Performance has flatlined since 2012 despite billions in education spending.
  • 2019: Teacher workload crisis. The National Union of the Teaching Profession (NUTP) publicly warned that teachers were spending more time on paperwork than teaching. The ministry promised reform. Teachers report no meaningful change.
  • 2023: Phone ban debate. After multiple viral incidents of students using phones in class, calls for a national phone ban gained traction. The ministry opted for “guidelines” rather than a directive, leaving enforcement to individual schools.
  • 2024: Graduate unemployment. Bank Negara and DOSM data showed that roughly 1 in 4 Malaysian graduates under 30 were either unemployed or underemployed. The education system is producing graduates the economy doesn’t need with skills the market doesn’t want.
  • Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013–2025. The blueprint promised to bring Malaysia into the “top third” of international education benchmarks by 2025. The deadline has passed. Malaysia is nowhere near the target.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

Fadhlina, your teachers are telling you — publicly, in national media — that they are losing their classrooms. Students are ruder. Less curious. Less engaged. And the system offers teachers nothing to fight back with.

Your response has been pilot programmes, photo ops, and vague promises about “21st-century learning.” None of it reaches the teacher standing in front of 38 teenagers who would rather watch a 15-second TikTok than learn anything.

You inherited a broken system. That’s true. But you promised to fix it. Two years in, the PISA scores haven’t moved, the teachers are more burned out than ever, and the curriculum still treats memorisation as mastery.

If PH wants to claim it governs differently, start with the one institution that touches every Malaysian family. Fund it properly. Free teachers from bureaucratic busywork. Implement a real digital literacy and device management framework. Reform the curriculum so it rewards thinking, not regurgitation.

Or keep doing what you’re doing — and watch an entire generation scroll its way to irrelevance.

Sources: FMT | PISA 2022 | World Bank — Malaysia Education | Malaysiakini

item.title: School Bullying Cases Surge 17% — 7,681 Cases in 2024 Despite 'Reforms'
item.description:
Said by: Fadhlina Sidek, Education Minister

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-18

Schools recorded 7,681 bullying cases in 2024, up from 6,528 in 2023 — a 17% increase. Teachers report students are ‘ruder, more restless, less curious.’ Education Ministry’s disciplinary reforms are clearly not working.

item.title: Education Minister Under Fire — 'System Failed Both Teachers and Students' Says Own PH Minister
item.description:
Said by: Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad / Fadhlina Sidek (Education Minister)

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-17

📌 What Happened

Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek faced open criticism from multiple quarters — including her own coalition colleague, Natural Resources Minister Nik Nazmi, who publicly stated that the ’education crisis stems from a system that failed both teachers and students.’ With 7,681 bullying cases in 2024 (up 17%), 10,160 SPM no-shows, 25% math failure rates, and 30% of SPM candidates failing at least one subject, the system is in freefall.

🔥 Why It Matters

When your own cabinet colleague publicly criticises your portfolio, it means the problem has become undeniable. The numbers are catastrophic: the best-performing subject in SPM is ‘Quran and Sunnah’ at 84% pass rate, while mathematics languishes at 75% and science at 74%. A system that produces religious knowledge more effectively than basic numeracy is not preparing students for the 21st century.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The government’s response has been cosmetic: remove exams (UPSR and PMR abolished), lower pass thresholds (20% reportedly passes math), and run communication campaigns. They’ve essentially hidden the problem by removing the measurement tools. As one analyst wrote: ’the journey seems to be just to promote incompetence from one stage to the other until students reach SPM.’ When you abolish failing tests instead of fixing failing schools, you’re choosing ignorance over reform.

📜 Previous Cases

The Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013-2025 was supposed to be the comprehensive solution. It was a beautifully designed document that changed almost nothing. Before that, there was the Education Development Master Plan 2006-2010. Before that, the Smart Schools initiative. Decades of ’transformation plans’ have produced Malaysia’s worst PISA results in history. The pattern is clear: announce plan → fund PR campaign → ignore implementation → announce new plan.

🎯 PH Callout

Your minister has been in the job since 2022. Results have gotten WORSE, not better. Instead of accountability, you give her photo opportunities. Instead of funding schools, you fund JAKIM (RM2.6B) and J-Kom propaganda (RM85.5M). Malaysian children deserve a world-class education, not a world-class communications plan about education. Fire your education minister or fix the system — preferably both.

Sources

  • The Star — Education Crisis
  • TheIns — How Malaysia’s Education Is Failing
  • Fulcrum — Think of Implementation

item.title: Chinese and Tamil Schools Used as Political Football Every Budget — Promised Millions, Delivered Scraps
item.description:
Said by: Dong Zong / Tamil Foundation / MCA / MIC

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-01

📌 What Happened

Every Malaysian budget cycle produces the same theatre: Chinese and Tamil school communities lobby desperately for maintenance, construction, and development funds. Politicians announce allocations with fanfare. Then the actual disbursement falls short, is delayed, or comes with conditions that limit use. The cycle repeats annually, with non-Malay communities performing gratitude for inadequate allocations.

🔥 Why It Matters

1.3 million students attend Chinese-medium schools (SJKC) and hundreds of thousands attend Tamil-medium schools (SJKT). These schools educate a significant portion of the non-Malay population and consistently outperform national schools in certain metrics. Yet they receive systematically less government funding per student, forcing communities to self-fund infrastructure that should be the government’s responsibility.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH, which depends heavily on Chinese and Indian votes, should have resolved this decades-old inequity. Instead, PH plays the same game as BN: use budget announcements as photo opportunities with Chinese and Indian community leaders, generate grateful headlines, then under-deliver. The structural disparity in per-student funding between national schools (SK) and vernacular schools remains because addressing it would require either more money (which the government claims it doesn’t have) or admitting that the current system is discriminatory (which it won’t).

📜 Previous Cases

BN used vernacular school allocations as political patronage — offering funds in exchange for community support. MCA and MIC existed primarily to secure these allocations, perpetuating dependency. PH was supposed to break this cycle by funding schools equitably regardless of medium of instruction. Instead, PH has maintained the BN model: Chinese and Indian schools get what PH decides to give them, when PH decides to give it.

🎯 PH Callout

Non-Malay Malaysians didn’t vote PH to continue BN’s patronage model with better optics. They voted for EQUAL treatment. Fund vernacular schools at the same per-student rate as national schools. Not as a favour. Not as a budget announcement photo op. As a constitutional obligation under Article 152 and basic educational equity. Or admit that ‘Madani for all’ has an asterisk: *terms and conditions apply based on ethnicity.

Sources

  • New Mandala — Rescuing Malaysian Higher Education
  • FMT — Decline of Malaysia’s Schooling System

item.title: Two-Thirds of Malaysian Universities DROP in Global Rankings — Funding Keeps Shrinking
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim Administration

Category: Education

Date: 2025-06-02

9 Malaysian universities in CWUR 2025, but 6 dropped in ranking. Higher education budget share declined from 6.8% (2010) to 4.3% (2025). You cannot claim ‘world-class education’ while cutting the budget that funds it.

item.title: Digital Divide Persists — Rural Communities Still Without Reliable Internet While Government Talks 5G
item.description:
Said by: MCMC / Rural Development Ministry

Category: Education

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: As of early 2025, millions of Malaysians in rural Sabah, Sarawak, and the east coast of Peninsular Malaysia still lack reliable internet access. While the government spent billions on the 5G DNB versus Telekom Malaysia dual-network fiasco, the JENDELA programme to improve basic digital infrastructure faced delays and coverage gaps. Students in rural schools were severely disadvantaged during COVID-19 distance learning, and the digital divide has not meaningfully narrowed since. MCMC’s own data shows connectivity gaps between urban and rural areas remain significant, with many rural schools relying on unstable satellite connections or no connectivity at all.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The digital divide compounds every other form of marginalisation. Rural students can’t access online learning resources. Small businesses can’t participate in e-commerce. Healthcare providers can’t use telemedicine. Government services that have moved online are inaccessible to communities without internet. Kids in longhouses and kampungs can’t load a basic web page while KL gets 5G. The irony of spending billions on cutting-edge infrastructure while basic connectivity remains unavailable to millions is not lost on anyone except apparently the government.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government chose to spend billions on a dual 5G network — solving a political problem between telecom companies — while JENDELA, the basic connectivity programme, faces delays. For Orang Asli and indigenous communities in particular, digital exclusion compounds existing educational and economic marginalisation. A government that prioritises 5G headlines over universal basic connectivity has its priorities backwards. A truly “Madani” government would ensure every Malaysian can access basic internet before giving KL residents faster streaming.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Digital infrastructure promises have been made under every Malaysian government. Under BN, the 1Malaysia Internet Centre programme was launched — many centres were built but poorly maintained or unstaffed. Under PH 1.0, digital infrastructure was briefly prioritised before the Sheraton Move. Under PH 2.0, JENDELA has continued but with persistent delays. The pattern is consistent: announce connectivity programmes, build some infrastructure, under-maintain it, and move on to the next headline technology while basic access gaps persist.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You spent billions on 5G so KL can stream faster. Meanwhile, a kid in a Sarawak longhouse can’t submit homework online. That’s not a digital divide — it’s digital apartheid. Prioritise JENDELA completion. Ensure every school in Malaysia has reliable broadband. Fix basic connectivity before chasing 5G headlines. Or keep building luxury infrastructure for urban areas while rural Malaysians — the communities that vote for you in every election — remain disconnected from the digital economy you keep promoting.

Sources: FMT

item.title: Higher Education Budget Keeps Shrinking — From 6.8% in 2010 to Just 4.3% in 2025
item.description:
Said by: New Mandala / Academic Analysis

Category: Education

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s higher education budget has dropped from 6.8% of the total national budget in 2010 to just 4.3% in 2025, with the lowest point at 3.9% in 2023. Meanwhile, tertiary enrollment stands at only 42.57% — far below Singapore’s 93% and the global average of 51.98%. Academic analysis describes a “widening disjuncture between reformist discourse and actual financial commitment” to education under PH. While the government talks about the knowledge economy and human capital development, the budget tells a different story.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A country that reduces education spending while talking about becoming a knowledge economy is lying to itself. The 4.3% allocation means fewer university places, less research funding, lower academic salaries, and deteriorating campus infrastructure. The tertiary enrollment gap — Malaysia at 42.57% vs Singapore at 93% — represents millions of young Malaysians who could be in higher education but aren’t, because the system doesn’t have the capacity to accommodate them. Every percentage point of budget decline translates into fewer graduates, less innovation, and slower economic growth.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The same government that cut higher education to 4.3% allocated RM2.6 billion to JAKIM — the highest allocation in JAKIM’s history. The civil service budget grows every year while education shrinks. When your budget priorities tell students they matter less than bureaucrats and religious administration, students respond by leaving the country. The brain drain — 1.86 million Malaysians abroad — is directly connected to an education system that receives declining investment. You can’t build human capital while cutting the budget for building it.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, education received a larger budget share but was plagued by inefficiency and political management. Under PH 1.0, education reform was briefly prioritised. Under PH 2.0, the budget has consistently declined. New Mandala’s analysis noted that PH’s education rhetoric is more progressive than any previous government but its funding commitment is among the worst. The gap between words and money is the gap between aspiration and reality.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You talk about human capital. You talk about the knowledge economy. You talk about innovation and competitiveness. Then you cut higher education to 4.3% of the budget — the lowest in modern Malaysian history. Singapore spends three times as much proportionally and has 93% tertiary enrollment. You have 42.57%. The numbers don’t lie. Reverse the education budget decline. Commit to reaching 6% within three years. Or stop talking about the knowledge economy while defunding the institutions that are supposed to build it.

Sources: New Mandala | Fulcrum | Taylor’s University

item.title: Malaysia Tertiary Enrollment at 42.57% — Below Global Average, Far Behind Singapore's 93%
item.description:
Said by: Academic Research / UNESCO Data

Category: Education

Date: 2025-01-01

Only 42.57% of eligible Malaysians enrolled in tertiary education — below global average of 51.98%. Singapore at 93%, US at 88%. Over 10,000 SPM students (2.6%) didn’t even sit for the exam.

item.title: PTPTN Loan Crisis — Millions Owe Billions, Repayment Rates Abysmal, Graduates Trapped in Debt
item.description:
Said by: PTPTN / Auditor General Reports

Category: Education

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 What Happened

The National Higher Education Fund Corporation (PTPTN) faces a chronic repayment crisis, with billions of ringgit in outstanding loans and repayment rates that consistently fall short of targets. Millions of Malaysian graduates carry PTPTN debt that limits their ability to save, invest, or purchase homes. The fund’s sustainability is questionable without major reform.

🔥 Why It Matters

PTPTN debt creates a generational poverty trap: graduates enter the workforce already indebted, face an economy with 12.28% youth unemployment, earn wages that barely cover living costs (RM1,500 minimum wage), and must somehow repay student loans while building a life. Many can’t — which is why repayment rates are so low. The cycle feeds itself: unaffordable education → debt → low wages → inability to repay → creditworthiness issues → inability to get mortgages → permanent renter status.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH promised various PTPTN reforms including debt relief for low-income graduates, extended repayment flexibility, and making education more affordable to reduce future borrowing. Progress has been marginal. The government talks about education as an investment but treats PTPTN as a debt collection agency. Students who borrowed to get degrees that the economy doesn’t value (because the education system doesn’t produce employable graduates) are being punished for a system-level failure that isn’t their fault.

📜 Previous Cases

BN’s approach was also inadequate — carrot-and-stick approaches to loan recovery without addressing the fundamental issues of education quality and labour market mismatch. PH 1.0 promised PTPTN reform but collapsed before implementation. PH 2.0 has made incremental adjustments but the structural crisis remains. The money lent produces graduates who can’t find matching employment, who then can’t repay loans, which undermines the fund’s ability to lend to future students. It’s a doom loop.

🎯 PH Callout

Your education system produces graduates who can’t find jobs (12.28% youth unemployment). Your economy doesn’t pay graduates enough to repay their loans (RM1,500 minimum wage). And your PTPTN continues lending for degrees that don’t lead to employment. You’ve created a perfectly designed poverty machine: borrow for education → get a degree → find no matching job → can’t repay loan → can’t buy house → stay in poverty. Fix the pipeline or stop pretending the problem is lazy graduates.

Sources

  • Taylor’s University — The Value of Education
  • FMT — Decline of Malaysia’s Schooling System

item.title: Teacher Shortage in Rural Schools — Kids in Kampungs Get Second-Class Education
item.description:
Said by: Education Ministry / NUTP

Category: Education

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Rural schools across Malaysia face chronic teacher shortages in mathematics, science, and English — the three subjects most critical for economic competitiveness. Indigenous communities in Sabah, Sarawak, and Orang Asli settlements face the worst gaps, with some schools lacking qualified teachers entirely for key subjects. The National Union of the Teaching Profession (NUTP) has repeatedly raised the issue. The Education Ministry acknowledges the shortage but has not implemented structural reforms to address it.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A child’s postcode determines the quality of education they receive. Urban schools in KL, Penang, and JB have qualified teachers, resources, and facilities. Rural schools — especially in Sabah, Sarawak, and Orang Asli areas — have empty classrooms, unqualified substitute teachers, and crumbling infrastructure. This two-tier system perpetuates generational poverty: rural children receive inferior education, score lower on SPM, access fewer university places, and earn less throughout their lives. The education gap is not a resource problem — it is a political priorities problem.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government spends RM2.6 billion on JAKIM and shrinks the higher education budget to 4.3%. Rural teacher shortages have been documented for decades. The solution is known: incentivise qualified teachers to serve in rural areas with housing, salary bonuses, and career advancement. Singapore does this. Thailand does this. Malaysia talks about it. Every education blueprint mentions rural schools. None solves the problem because solving it requires redirecting money from urban constituencies to rural areas — and urban constituencies have more political leverage.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Rural teacher shortages have been a known problem since the 1980s. Under BN, the “Guru Cemerlang” programme was supposed to attract top teachers to underserved areas — it didn’t. Under PH 1.0, rural education was briefly prioritised before the Sheraton Move. Under PH 2.0, the same shortage persists. Each government launches a programme with a new name. None provides the structural incentives — housing, salary, career progression — that would actually convince qualified teachers to serve in rural Sabah or Orang Asli settlements.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Kids in kampungs deserve the same quality teachers as kids in KL. They don’t get them because your government won’t pay for it. Provide housing and salary incentives for rural teachers. Build partnerships with local universities to train teachers for their own communities. Fund rural school infrastructure to the same standard as urban schools. Or keep producing two classes of Malaysian graduates — one educated, one abandoned — and wonder why the development gap never closes.

Sources: FMT | NUTP | Fulcrum

item.title: PISA Scores Collapse — Malaysia Loses 1.6 YEARS of Learning, Now 8 Years Behind Singapore in Math
item.description:
Said by: OECD PISA 2022 Report / ISIS Malaysia Analysis

Category: Education

Date: 2024-12-06

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: The 2022 PISA report, released in December 2024, revealed Malaysia suffered among the top five largest declines globally across all three subjects. Mathematics dropped 31 points (440 to 409), reading fell 27 points (415 to 388), and science declined 22 points (438 to 416). ISIS Malaysia estimated this equates to 1.6 years of lost learning in mathematics alone. 59% of Malaysian students are now below basic proficiency in math — up from 41%. Only 1% of Malaysian students achieved high proficiency. The socioeconomic gap is widening, with wealthy students outperforming disadvantaged ones by 82 points.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Malaysia is now 8 years behind Singapore and 3 years behind Vietnam in mathematics educational attainment. This isn’t just a statistic — it’s a generation of young Malaysians who cannot compete in the global knowledge economy. The decline is not evenly distributed: rural students, B40 families, and students in underperforming states bear the worst impact. When 59% of your 15-year-olds can’t do basic math, the consequences ripple through the economy for decades — in lower productivity, higher unemployment, and reduced innovation capacity.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government blamed COVID-19 school closures, but the OECD average only dropped 15 points in math — Malaysia dropped double at 31 points. Other countries with similar closures performed better. The decline predates COVID: Malaysia has been falling since 2015. The pandemic accelerated a rot that was already there — underfunded schools, poorly trained teachers, and a curriculum that prioritises rote memorisation over critical thinking. Malaysia pulled out of PISA entirely in 2003 and 2006 because the results were embarrassing. The Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013-2025 was supposed to fix everything — it fixed nothing. A decade of “blueprints” and “transformation plans” produced the worst results in history.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, Malaysia maintained mediocre but stable PISA scores. Under PH, scores collapsed. The percentage of students below basic proficiency increased from 41% to 59%. Every education minister launches a blueprint. None delivers improvement. The fundamental problem — a curriculum designed around political and ethnic considerations rather than educational excellence — remains untouched because no government has been willing to pay the political cost of genuine reform. JAKIM receives RM2.6 billion. Higher education gets 4.3% of the budget.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your own minister admitted the system “failed both teachers and students.” You allocate RM2.6 billion to JAKIM for religious affairs but cut higher education to 4.3% of the budget. Your students are 8 years behind Singapore. 59% can’t do basic math. Only 1% achieve high proficiency. Your priorities are clear — and education isn’t one of them. Reverse the education budget decline. Invest in teacher quality. Reform the curriculum for critical thinking. Or keep producing the worst PISA results in Malaysian history and wonder why the brain drain accelerates.

Sources: ISIS Malaysia | Fulcrum

item.title: 30% of SPM Candidates FAIL At Least One Subject — A Pass Grade of 20% in Math Is Not Education, It's a Lie
item.description:
Said by: Education Ministry Data / Independent Analysis

Category: Education

Date: 2024-05-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In SPM 2023 results released in May 2024, approximately 30% of candidates failed at least one subject. Mathematics had the highest failure rate at 25.9%, followed by science at 25%. The best-performing subject was Quran and Sunnah at 84%. Reports indicate the pass threshold for mathematics may be as low as 20% — meaning a student who gets 4 out of 5 questions wrong still “passes.” The government had previously abolished UPSR (Year 6) and PMR (Year 9) examinations, removing the only diagnostic checkpoints that could identify struggling students early.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When you set the pass mark at 20%, you’re not measuring education — you’re manufacturing the illusion of it. A student who “passes” mathematics with a 20% score cannot do basic arithmetic reliably. These students enter the workforce, attempt further education, or become statistics in the 12.28% youth unemployment figure. The education system is producing graduates with certificates but without competence. Under BN, Malaysia maintained mediocre but stable PISA scores (440 in math in 2018). Under PH, scores crashed 31 points to 409. The percentage of students below basic proficiency increased from 41% to 59%.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government abolished UPSR and PMR — removing the diagnostic checkpoints that would catch struggling students at ages 12 and 15 — then acts surprised when 30% fail at SPM. As one analyst put it: “The journey seems to be just to promote incompetence from one stage to the other until students reach SPM.” When the government removes tests and lowers pass marks, it’s not reforming education — it’s hiding failure. The education system is producing religious knowledge better than mathematics, science, or English — and the government’s response is more blueprints, more jargon, and more press conferences.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013-2025, continued by PH, has been a decade-long failure by its own metrics. PISA scores have declined. SPM failure rates remain high. Youth unemployment suggests the system doesn’t prepare students for work. Every education minister launches a “transformation.” None transforms anything. The fundamental problem — a curriculum designed around political and ethnic considerations rather than educational excellence — remains untouched because reforming it would require confronting interests no government has been willing to challenge.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your education system produces religious knowledge better than mathematics. Your students are 8 years behind Singapore and 3 years behind Vietnam. You abolished the tests that would have caught this earlier. You lowered the pass mark to hide the failure. And you call this “Madani civilisation”? A civilisation that can’t teach its children to count is not a civilisation — it’s a civilisational collapse in slow motion. Restore diagnostic checkpoints. Raise pass standards to meaningful levels. Reform the curriculum for competence, not compliance. Or keep producing SPM certificates that employers don’t trust and the world doesn’t recognise.

Sources: TheIns | Fulcrum | ERIC

item.title: Over 10,000 SPM Students Didn't Even Sit for the Exam — Education System Haemorrhaging
item.description:
Said by: Education Ministry / DOSM

Category: Education

Date: 2024-03-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: More than 10,160 registered SPM candidates — 2.6% of the total — did not sit for Malaysia’s most important school examination in 2023. This is not a rounding error; it represents over ten thousand young Malaysians who disengaged from the education system entirely. Combined with a tertiary enrollment rate of just 42.57% (versus Singapore’s 93% and the global average of 51.98%), youth unemployment stuck at 12.28%, a higher education budget share that dropped from 6.8% in 2010 to 4.3% in 2025, and 7,681 reported bullying cases, the system is haemorrhaging students at every level.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When over 10,000 students don’t even show up for SPM, it signals a system that has lost legitimacy in the eyes of the people it’s supposed to serve. These are not students who failed — they are students who decided the system wasn’t worth showing up for. The reasons are multi-layered: financial hardship forcing teenagers into the workforce, mental health struggles, disillusionment with an education system perceived as irrelevant, and the growing sense that a Malaysian degree may not lead to meaningful employment given 12.28% youth unemployment.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government allocated a declining share of the national budget to education — down to 4.3% for higher education in 2025 from 6.8% in 2010. When the Education Minister reports 7,681 bullying cases and rising student disengagement, the response should be structural reform, not communication plans. But structural reform requires money and political will — both of which are directed elsewhere. The civil service budget grows every year. The education budget shrinks. When your budget priorities tell students they matter less than bureaucrats, students respond by walking away.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Student dropout and disengagement have been rising in Malaysia for years. The COVID pandemic accelerated the trend — thousands of students from B40 families never returned to school after lockdowns. The government’s response has been programmes and initiatives that generate press releases but don’t address root causes: poverty, irrelevant curricula, teacher quality, and a qualification system that employers don’t value. Every education minister announces a “transformation.” None transforms anything.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Ten thousand students gave up on your education system. They didn’t fail — they decided it wasn’t worth showing up for. When your budget for education keeps shrinking while your budget for civil service salaries keeps growing, you’re telling the next generation exactly where your priorities are. And they’re responding by walking away. Reverse the education budget decline. Reform the curriculum for relevance. Address bullying as a systemic crisis, not a statistic. Or keep losing students — and wonder why the brain drain starts before they even graduate.

Sources: Taylor’s University | FMT

item.title: Malaysia's PISA Scores Decline Most Among Southeast Asian Countries — Anwar Says 'Cannot Be in Denial'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Education

Date: 2024-01-15

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s 2022 PISA results — measuring education quality among 15-year-olds — showed the biggest decline among all Southeast Asian countries tested. Scores dropped across mathematics, science, and reading. Anwar responded by saying Malaysia “must know the state of our affairs and cannot be in a state of denial.” The Education Minister admitted the system “failed both teachers and students.” CNA reported that politics — particularly the tension between Malay language requirements and English proficiency — is behind the difficulty of education reform. Three years later, experts say nothing has changed on the ground.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Education determines a nation’s future. Malaysia’s education system is failing — and the government knows it. PISA results have stagnated for three consecutive assessment cycles while regional competitors like Vietnam and Indonesia have improved. FMT observed: “Every few years, the ministry launches a new education blueprint. It’s got a sleek logo and pages of jargon. But on the ground, nothing changes.” The gap between Malaysia and Singapore in mathematics is now equivalent to approximately 8 years of learning. A generation of Malaysian students is being left behind by a system that prioritises political considerations over educational outcomes.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: “Cannot be in denial” is an admission, not a policy. Anwar acknowledged the problem in January 2024. It is now March 2026. Where are the results? The politics of language, religion, and race make education reform nearly impossible in Malaysia’s coalition system — and PH has shown zero willingness to confront those politics. Mandatory Malay and History requirements across all education systems were expanded rather than reformed. UUCA — which restricts student political participation — remains intact despite being a manifesto promise. The education system is failing because fixing it requires confronting entrenched interests that PH depends on for political survival.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s education decline is not new. PISA scores have been mediocre since Malaysia first participated in 2009. Every education minister launches a new blueprint: the National Education Blueprint 2013-2025, various STEM initiatives, TVET expansions. Each generates reports and press conferences. None generates measurable improvement in student outcomes. The fundamental problem — a system designed around political and ethnic considerations rather than educational excellence — remains untouched because no government has been willing to pay the political cost of reforming it.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You said Malaysia “cannot be in denial.” Then you stayed in denial. PISA scores are declining. The education gap with Singapore is 8 years of learning. Your response was to acknowledge the problem and do nothing about it. Reform the curriculum to prioritise critical thinking over rote memorisation. Invest in teacher training and compensation. Address the language policy bottleneck honestly. Or keep launching education blueprints with sleek logos while Malaysian students fall further behind their regional peers — and wonder why the brain drain accelerates.

Sources: CNA | FMT
